--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Relative Size Comparison) ---
- PRE-CALCULATED spatial relationships reflect apparent image-plane size, not physical size. Objects of the same category at different depths—such as cows in a field, dancers in rows, or square tops on a surface—often appear different sizes due to perspective, but are physically similar. Same-category animals in a herd or group typically have only minor individual size variation; 'mostly similar' is usually more accurate than 'all different.'
- A foreground object that visually overlaps a distant large object cannot physically cover it. The scale gap between nearby small objects and distant large objects is usually enormous. Flowers from a small tub could cover only a tiny branch of a background tree—not half, not most, just a small part. Always estimate the physical scale ratio before choosing among graded coverage options.
- A chain, belt, or rope that wraps around the outside of a wheel has a larger effective radius than the wheel itself, because its path lies beyond the wheel's rim. When PRE-CALCULATED data says the chain is 'smaller,' it is reporting apparent image size, not path radius. The enclosing loop's radius is always greater than the enclosed object's radius.
- Curved transparent containers distort the apparent size of objects inside them. A spherical fishbowl magnifies contents in all directions due to its convex curvature. A cylindrical bowl stretches contents horizontally but not vertically. A flat-sided box-shaped container produces no magnification. When asked which shape makes fish 'look bigger,' cylindrical stretches shape while spherical enlarges overall appearance.
- Moving an object farther from the viewer makes it look smaller; moving it closer makes it look larger. When a question asks 'if object A is taken to object B's location, how will A look compared to C,' recompute A's apparent size at the new depth. A deer moved from the foreground to a distant bin's location will appear smaller than nearby objects.
- A vehicle's interior cargo space minus already-loaded items equals available space. If a van is partly filled with water bottles, the remaining free space may be too small for a puppy even though the entire van is larger than the puppy. When asked 'will X fit inside Y,' subtract existing contents before comparing.
- A shorter background set or backdrop makes the room in front of it appear more expanded and spacious, because the low backdrop reveals more of the actual room behind and above it. It does not make the room look smaller.
- Biological structures have growth limits. A mature flower's inner parts will not grow beyond the outer petals—they remain as they are. Shoes of different sizes differ not only in length but also in width, instep height, and toe box volume. A bird's neck can appear short when folded but extend to considerable length when stretched.
- Physical strength and structural capacity matter for load-bearing questions. A tree, no matter how tall, cannot lift a concrete building—the tree would be crushed under the building's weight. A human body (~1.7 m long) is far too short to span a river or canal that is typically several meters wide. A lighthouse laid horizontally would likely not span a wide canal, and even if it did, it lacks the structural design to serve as a walkable bridge.
- Printed graphics, patterns, or illustrations on a bag, box, or surface create visual illusions of size. A bag with a large book illustration printed on it looks bigger than it actually is. The bag's true internal volume is determined by its physical dimensions, not by the size of its printed decoration. When an option says 'the bag looks bigger due to the image on it,' it is identifying this illusion.
- When comparing an aggregate of smaller objects to a single larger object, mentally reconstruct both at the same depth. A large red stone can be slightly larger than the combined length of three smaller stones stacked above it. Different fruits cut in half have different cross-sectional areas from each other and from whole fruits—the answer is 'different areas for different items,' not a simple true/false about halving.
- Manufactured items of the same specification—square panel tops, table segments, stair treads—are physically identical even when they appear different due to perspective. When asked 'are all X the same size,' and the items are clearly from the same production or construction set, default to 'all equal' unless there is explicit evidence of mixed types.
- When people are sitting, kneeling, or in dance poses, their standing height cannot be determined from their current image height. If the question asks 'will row 1 be the same height as row 2 when standing,' and there is insufficient information about their true standing heights, prefer 'unable to determine' over a definitive yes or no.
- When a table or rectangular object is rotated 90 degrees, its former width becomes its new 'length.' Two tables of the same dimensions oriented differently can have the same length after one is turned. Do not conclude they differ just because they appear different in their current orientations.

1299.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Scale Consistency) ---
- Whether an object is appropriately sized for a space depends on whether the object's functional coverage matches the space's requirements. When an appliance, fixture, or piece of furniture's serving range is clearly smaller than the space it is meant to serve—such as a small chandelier in a grand hall or a single small air conditioner in a large restaurant—judge it as 'needs to be larger,' not 'appropriate.'
- Text legibility on a sign or poster depends on the joint scale of the display surface and the font size as projected to the viewer's eye. Making the entire poster larger—not just walking closer or enlarging only the font—is often the correct remedy when text is too small to read at the intended viewing distance.
- A container's carrying capacity depends on its three-dimensional internal volume—depth, width, and opening—not just its visible footprint or shape. A deep narrow basket can hold far more than a wide shallow bowl. Differently shaped containers such as triangular versus circular boxes cannot be compared without knowing internal volume. When volume data is unavailable, the comparison may be non-measurable.
- For seating capacity, estimate each table's perimeter and divide by roughly 60 cm per person. A long banquet table seats 5+ per side. Two such tables easily accommodate 10+ people. Do not default to small fixed numbers like 4 per table without visual evidence of a small table.
- A baby or toddler is physically small and light enough to sit on a medium-to-large dog from a pure size-and-scale standpoint. A tree's canopy of leaves inflates its visual bulk far beyond its trunk's solid mass. A bird's retractable neck appears short when folded but extends to considerable length. These are common biological scale facts that override naive visual impressions.
- Industrially manufactured items on the same production line—spools, rollers, bobbins, cable drums, water dispensers, dinnerware sets—are almost always identical in size. Apparent differences in images are caused by perspective, angle, or occlusion. Default to 'same size' unless there is explicit evidence of mixed product types or different batches.
- Stairs that appear smaller at the top of a staircase are the same physical size as the bottom steps; the shrinkage is perspective. Fruits of the same variety at the same stall are typically sorted to similar sizes. Miniature scenes and model displays use artificially scaled props—do not treat their proportions as representing real-world scale relationships.
- When a question asks whether an object can 'fit inside' another, consider all possible placements: inside, on a shelf, on top, or on a front ledge. Read all answer options to identify the specific placement described. For stacking compatibility of appliances, kitchen and laundry machines follow standardized dimension ranges and are often stackable.
- A background or backdrop larger than the foreground object typically makes the foreground object appear more prominent and visually bigger through framing contrast—not smaller. A road's spaciousness depends on designed lane capacity, not momentary traffic congestion. Cloud coverage over a field depends on dynamic wind and weather factors not determinable from a single photo.
- A standard sedan is roughly 2–3 times the length of a scooter. Sugarcane juice yield depends on cross-sectional thickness, not just length. Sweets and pastries can be shaped to dimensions comparable to common household objects like drinking glasses. If all identical objects are scaled up by the same factor, their relative size ordering is preserved—equals remain equal.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Distance-Size Correlation) ---
- Image size is jointly determined by physical size and distance from the camera. A physically smaller person standing closer to the viewpoint can appear the same height as a taller person standing farther away. When a question asks whether repositioning would equalize appearance, the answer depends on how much the depth ratio compensates for the true size ratio.
- Repeated architectural or manufactured elements arranged in depth—such as windows on successive floors, pipes in a row, lamp posts along a road, or cart statues in a plaza—are almost always the same physical size. Their apparent size shrinks with distance purely because of perspective foreshortening, not because the far ones are actually smaller.
- When a question asks 'will object A look the same size as object B if seen from close,' it is asking about projected appearance after hypothetical relocation. If both objects are the same category and type, moving to the same viewing distance will usually reveal equal physical size.
- Distinguish between 'looks larger' and 'is larger.' Many questions in this domain test whether the model understands that apparent size changes with viewpoint while physical size remains constant. If the answer options mix perceptual and physical language, prefer the interpretation most consistent with the option wording.
- When two children or people of slightly different heights stand at different distances, the shorter one can be made to appear as tall as the taller one by placing the shorter one closer. This is a direct consequence of linear perspective and does not require any optical illusion.

--- Domain Rules (Relative Size Comparison) ---

Objects of the same category at different depths—such as cows in a field, dancers in rows, or square tops on a surface—often appear different sizes due to perspective, but are physically similar. Flowers from a small tub could cover only a tiny branch of a background tree—not half, not most, just a small part. Always estimate the physical scale ratio before choosing among graded coverage options.

A cylindrical bowl stretches contents horizontally but not vertically. A flat-sided box-shaped container produces no magnification. When asked which shape makes fish 'look bigger,' cylindrical stretches shape while spherical enlarges overall appearance.

1310.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Scale Consistency) ---
- Whether an object is appropriately sized for a space depends on whether the object's functional coverage matches the space's requirements. When an appliance, fixture, or piece of furniture's serving range is clearly smaller than the space it is meant to serve—such as a small chandelier in a grand hall or a single small air conditioner in a large restaurant—judge it as 'needs to be larger,' not 'appropriate.'
- Text legibility on a sign or poster depends on the joint scale of the display surface and the font size as projected to the viewer's eye. Making the entire poster larger—not just walking closer or enlarging only the font—is often the correct remedy when text is too small to read at the intended viewing distance.
- A container's carrying capacity depends on its three-dimensional internal volume—depth, width, and opening—not just its visible footprint or shape. A deep narrow basket can hold far more than a wide shallow bowl. Differently shaped containers such as triangular versus circular boxes cannot be compared without knowing internal volume. When volume data is unavailable, the comparison may be non-measurable.
- For seating capacity, estimate each table's perimeter and divide by roughly 60 cm per person. A long banquet table seats 5+ per side. Two such tables easily accommodate 10+ people. Do not default to small fixed numbers like 4 per table without visual evidence of a small table.
- A baby or toddler is physically small and light enough to sit on a medium-to-large dog from a pure size-and-scale standpoint. A tree's canopy of leaves inflates its visual bulk far beyond its trunk's solid mass. A bird's retractable neck appears short when folded but extends to considerable length. These are common biological scale facts that override naive visual impressions.
- Industrially manufactured items on the same production line—spools, rollers, bobbins, cable drums, water dispensers, dinnerware sets—are almost always identical in size. Apparent differences in images are caused by perspective, angle, or occlusion. Default to 'same size' unless there is explicit evidence of mixed product types or different batches.
- Stairs that appear smaller at the top of a staircase are the same physical size as the bottom steps; the shrinkage is perspective. Fruits of the same variety at the same stall are typically sorted to similar sizes. Miniature scenes and model displays use artificially scaled props—do not treat their proportions as representing real-world scale relationships.
- When a question asks whether an object can 'fit inside' another, consider all possible placements: inside, on a shelf, on top, or on a front ledge. Read all answer options to identify the specific placement described. For stacking compatibility of appliances, kitchen and laundry machines follow standardized dimension ranges and are often stackable.
- A background or backdrop larger than the foreground object typically makes the foreground object appear more prominent and visually bigger through framing contrast—not smaller. A road's spaciousness depends on designed lane capacity, not momentary traffic congestion. Cloud coverage over a field depends on dynamic wind and weather factors not determinable from a single photo.
- A standard sedan is roughly 2–3 times the length of a scooter. Sugarcane juice yield depends on cross-sectional thickness, not just length. Sweets and pastries can be shaped to dimensions comparable to common household objects like drinking glasses. If all identical objects are scaled up by the same factor, their relative size ordering is preserved—equals remain equal.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Shadow-Size Projection) ---
- A shadow's size on a receiving surface is not a simple copy of the casting object's size. It depends on three factors: the distance from the light source to the object, the distance from the object to the surface, and the angle of incidence. Moving an object closer to the light source enlarges its shadow; moving it farther away shrinks it.
- When a shadow is cast onto a surface that is not parallel to the object's plane—such as a wall receiving a shadow from a horizontal wire, or a floor receiving a shadow from a vertical window grille—the shadow is typically elongated or distorted rather than being a same-scale silhouette.
- Outdoor shadows from a low sun angle are almost always longer than the casting object's height or width. The lower the sun, the more elongated the shadow becomes. Overhead midday sun produces shadows that are shorter than the object.
- Tall buildings cast wide shadow regions that can darken large areas of ground and neighboring structures. The shadow area of a tall building is determined by both its height and the sun's elevation angle, so a moderately wide but very tall building can still cast a shadow broad enough to shade an entire street.
- Window-gap and grille projections onto interior walls or floors are commonly enlarged or stretched near one edge because the light enters at an oblique angle. The shadow pattern at the top or far edge of the projection is typically wider than the physical gap that created it.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Shadow-Size Projection) ---
- A shadow's size on a receiving surface is not a simple copy of the casting object's size. It depends on three factors: the distance from the light source to the object, the distance from the object to the surface, and the angle of incidence. Moving an object closer to the light source enlarges its shadow; moving it farther away shrinks it.
- When a shadow is cast onto a surface that is not parallel to the object's plane—such as a wall receiving a shadow from a horizontal wire, or a floor receiving a shadow from a vertical window grille—the shadow is typically elongated or distorted rather than being a same-scale silhouette.
- Outdoor shadows from a low sun angle are almost always longer than the casting object's height or width. The lower the sun, the more elongated the shadow becomes. Overhead midday sun produces shadows that are shorter than the object.
- Tall buildings cast wide shadow regions that can darken large areas of ground and neighboring structures. The shadow area of a tall building is determined by both its height and the sun's elevation angle, so a moderately wide but very tall building can still cast a shadow broad enough to shade an entire street.
- Window-gap and grille projections onto interior walls or floors are commonly enlarged or stretched near one edge because the light enters at an oblique angle. The shadow pattern at the top or far edge of the projection is typically wider than the physical gap that created it.

1333.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Scale Consistency) ---
- Whether an object is appropriately sized for a space depends on whether the object's functional coverage matches the space's requirements. When an appliance, fixture, or piece of furniture's serving range is clearly smaller than the space it is meant to serve—such as a small chandelier in a grand hall or a single small air conditioner in a large restaurant—judge it as 'needs to be larger,' not 'appropriate.'
- Text legibility on a sign or poster depends on the joint scale of the display surface and the font size as projected to the viewer's eye. Making the entire poster larger—not just walking closer or enlarging only the font—is often the correct remedy when text is too small to read at the intended viewing distance.
- A container's carrying capacity depends on its three-dimensional internal volume—depth, width, and opening—not just its visible footprint or shape. A deep narrow basket can hold far more than a wide shallow bowl. Differently shaped containers such as triangular versus circular boxes cannot be compared without knowing internal volume. When volume data is unavailable, the comparison may be non-measurable.
- For seating capacity, estimate each table's perimeter and divide by roughly 60 cm per person. A long banquet table seats 5+ per side. Two such tables easily accommodate 10+ people. Do not default to small fixed numbers like 4 per table without visual evidence of a small table.
- A baby or toddler is physically small and light enough to sit on a medium-to-large dog from a pure size-and-scale standpoint. A tree's canopy of leaves inflates its visual bulk far beyond its trunk's solid mass. A bird's retractable neck appears short when folded but extends to considerable length. These are common biological scale facts that override naive visual impressions.
- Industrially manufactured items on the same production line—spools, rollers, bobbins, cable drums, water dispensers, dinnerware sets—are almost always identical in size. Apparent differences in images are caused by perspective, angle, or occlusion. Default to 'same size' unless there is explicit evidence of mixed product types or different batches.
- Stairs that appear smaller at the top of a staircase are the same physical size as the bottom steps; the shrinkage is perspective. Fruits of the same variety at the same stall are typically sorted to similar sizes. Miniature scenes and model displays use artificially scaled props—do not treat their proportions as representing real-world scale relationships.
- When a question asks whether an object can 'fit inside' another, consider all possible placements: inside, on a shelf, on top, or on a front ledge. Read all answer options to identify the specific placement described. For stacking compatibility of appliances, kitchen and laundry machines follow standardized dimension ranges and are often stackable.
- A background or backdrop larger than the foreground object typically makes the foreground object appear more prominent and visually bigger through framing contrast—not smaller. A road's spaciousness depends on designed lane capacity, not momentary traffic congestion. Cloud coverage over a field depends on dynamic wind and weather factors not determinable from a single photo.
- A standard sedan is roughly 2–3 times the length of a scooter. Sugarcane juice yield depends on cross-sectional thickness, not just length. Sweets and pastries can be shaped to dimensions comparable to common household objects like drinking glasses. If all identical objects are scaled up by the same factor, their relative size ordering is preserved—equals remain equal.

1345.spatial_relations_text:
=== Size & Height Deductions ===
- 'Mountains' is SHORTER/SMALLER than 'Carts'.
- 'Carts' is SHORTER/SMALLER than 'Sky'.

=== Horizontal Level Alignment ===

=== Occlusion & Depth Deductions ===

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Distance-Size Correlation) ---
- Image size is jointly determined by physical size and distance from the camera. A physically smaller person standing closer to the viewpoint can appear the same height as a taller person standing farther away. When a question asks whether repositioning would equalize appearance, the answer depends on how much the depth ratio compensates for the true size ratio.
- Repeated architectural or manufactured elements arranged in depth—such as windows on successive floors, pipes in a row, lamp posts along a road, or cart statues in a plaza—are almost always the same physical size. Their apparent size shrinks with distance purely because of perspective foreshortening, not because the far ones are actually smaller.
- When a question asks 'will object A look the same size as object B if seen from close,' it is asking about projected appearance after hypothetical relocation. If both objects are the same category and type, moving to the same viewing distance will usually reveal equal physical size.
- Distinguish between 'looks larger' and 'is larger.' Many questions in this domain test whether the model understands that apparent size changes with viewpoint while physical size remains constant. If the answer options mix perceptual and physical language, prefer the interpretation most consistent with the option wording.
- When two children or people of slightly different heights stand at different distances, the shorter one can be made to appear as tall as the taller one by placing the shorter one closer. This is a direct consequence of linear perspective and does not require any optical illusion.

1393.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Scale Consistency) ---
- Whether an object is appropriately sized for a space depends on whether the object's functional coverage matches the space's requirements. When an appliance, fixture, or piece of furniture's serving range is clearly smaller than the space it is meant to serve—such as a small chandelier in a grand hall or a single small air conditioner in a large restaurant—judge it as 'needs to be larger,' not 'appropriate.'
- Text legibility on a sign or poster depends on the joint scale of the display surface and the font size as projected to the viewer's eye. Making the entire poster larger—not just walking closer or enlarging only the font—is often the correct remedy when text is too small to read at the intended viewing distance.
- A container's carrying capacity depends on its three-dimensional internal volume—depth, width, and opening—not just its visible footprint or shape. A deep narrow basket can hold far more than a wide shallow bowl. Differently shaped containers such as triangular versus circular boxes cannot be compared without knowing internal volume. When volume data is unavailable, the comparison may be non-measurable.
- For seating capacity, estimate each table's perimeter and divide by roughly 60 cm per person. A long banquet table seats 5+ per side. Two such tables easily accommodate 10+ people. Do not default to small fixed numbers like 4 per table without visual evidence of a small table.
- A baby or toddler is physically small and light enough to sit on a medium-to-large dog from a pure size-and-scale standpoint. A tree's canopy of leaves inflates its visual bulk far beyond its trunk's solid mass. A bird's retractable neck appears short when folded but extends to considerable length. These are common biological scale facts that override naive visual impressions.
- Industrially manufactured items on the same production line—spools, rollers, bobbins, cable drums, water dispensers, dinnerware sets—are almost always identical in size. Apparent differences in images are caused by perspective, angle, or occlusion. Default to 'same size' unless there is explicit evidence of mixed product types or different batches.
- Stairs that appear smaller at the top of a staircase are the same physical size as the bottom steps; the shrinkage is perspective. Fruits of the same variety at the same stall are typically sorted to similar sizes. Miniature scenes and model displays use artificially scaled props—do not treat their proportions as representing real-world scale relationships.
- When a question asks whether an object can 'fit inside' another, consider all possible placements: inside, on a shelf, on top, or on a front ledge. Read all answer options to identify the specific placement described. For stacking compatibility of appliances, kitchen and laundry machines follow standardized dimension ranges and are often stackable.
- A background or backdrop larger than the foreground object typically makes the foreground object appear more prominent and visually bigger through framing contrast—not smaller. A road's spaciousness depends on designed lane capacity, not momentary traffic congestion. Cloud coverage over a field depends on dynamic wind and weather factors not determinable from a single photo.
- A standard sedan is roughly 2–3 times the length of a scooter. Sugarcane juice yield depends on cross-sectional thickness, not just length. Sweets and pastries can be shaped to dimensions comparable to common household objects like drinking glasses. If all identical objects are scaled up by the same factor, their relative size ordering is preserved—equals remain equal.

=== Size & Height Deductions ===

=== Horizontal Level Alignment ===

=== Occlusion & Depth Deductions ===

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Shadow-Size Projection) ---
- A shadow's size on a receiving surface is not a simple copy of the casting object's size. It depends on three factors: the distance from the light source to the object, the distance from the object to the surface, and the angle of incidence. Moving an object closer to the light source enlarges its shadow; moving it farther away shrinks it.
- When a shadow is cast onto a surface that is not parallel to the object's plane—such as a wall receiving a shadow from a horizontal wire, or a floor receiving a shadow from a vertical window grille—the shadow is typically elongated or distorted rather than being a same-scale silhouette.
- Outdoor shadows from a low sun angle are almost always longer than the casting object's height or width. The lower the sun, the more elongated the shadow becomes. Overhead midday sun produces shadows that are shorter than the object.
- Tall buildings cast wide shadow regions that can darken large areas of ground and neighboring structures. The shadow area of a tall building is determined by both its height and the sun's elevation angle, so a moderately wide but very tall building can still cast a shadow broad enough to shade an entire street.
- Window-gap and grille projections onto interior walls or floors are commonly enlarged or stretched near one edge because the light enters at an oblique angle. The shadow pattern at the top or far edge of the projection is typically wider than the physical gap that created it.

=== Occlusion & Depth Deductions ===
- [Occlusion]: 'Sunglasses' is IN FRONT OF and PARTIALLY OCCLUDES 'Street'.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Perspective Distortion) ---
- Perspective and contextual contrast can change perceived size without changing true size. A human standing next to a giant sculpture or mural appears small by comparison, but remains physically standard-sized. When asked 'is this human smaller than actual size,' the correct answer usually acknowledges that the human is normal-sized and only the reference object is unusually large.
- When small objects of the same type are grouped together, a standard-sized object placed among them will appear larger by contrast. Conversely, a small object placed among large ones will appear even smaller. This is the Ebbinghaus illusion and it affects perceived—not actual—size.
- A shape viewed under perspective foreshortening can appear trapezoidal even if it is actually rectangular or square. A cart roof, table top, or tile pattern that looks like a trapezoid in a photograph may be a rectangle in reality, distorted only by the camera's viewpoint.
- When identical clips, cups, bottles, or tiles are arranged at different positions in the image, those closer to the camera or at image edges may appear larger. Before concluding a true size difference, check whether the objects are the same product or mold. If they are, the size difference is apparent, not real.
- Front-row members in a group photo, parade, or formation appear taller and larger than back-row members purely because they are closer to the camera. This does not mean front-row individuals are actually taller. Perspective exaggerates the size of nearer subjects relative to farther ones.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Shadow-Size Projection) ---
- A shadow's size on a receiving surface is not a simple copy of the casting object's size. It depends on three factors: the distance from the light source to the object, the distance from the object to the surface, and the angle of incidence. Moving an object closer to the light source enlarges its shadow; moving it farther away shrinks it.
- When a shadow is cast onto a surface that is not parallel to the object's plane—such as a wall receiving a shadow from a horizontal wire, or a floor receiving a shadow from a vertical window grille—the shadow is typically elongated or distorted rather than being a same-scale silhouette.
- Outdoor shadows from a low sun angle are almost always longer than the casting object's height or width. The lower the sun, the more elongated the shadow becomes. Overhead midday sun produces shadows that are shorter than the object.
- Tall buildings cast wide shadow regions that can darken large areas of ground and neighboring structures. The shadow area of a tall building is determined by both its height and the sun's elevation angle, so a moderately wide but very tall building can still cast a shadow broad enough to shade an entire street.
- Window-gap and grille projections onto interior walls or floors are commonly enlarged or stretched near one edge because the light enters at an oblique angle. The shadow pattern at the top or far edge of the projection is typically wider than the physical gap that created it.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Distance-Size Correlation) ---
- Image size is jointly determined by physical size and distance from the camera. A physically smaller person standing closer to the viewpoint can appear the same height as a taller person standing farther away. When a question asks whether repositioning would equalize appearance, the answer depends on how much the depth ratio compensates for the true size ratio.
- Repeated architectural or manufactured elements arranged in depth—such as windows on successive floors, pipes in a row, lamp posts along a road, or cart statues in a plaza—are almost always the same physical size. Their apparent size shrinks with distance purely because of perspective foreshortening, not because the far ones are actually smaller.
- When a question asks 'will object A look the same size as object B if seen from close,' it is asking about projected appearance after hypothetical relocation. If both objects are the same category and type, moving to the same viewing distance will usually reveal equal physical size.
- Distinguish between 'looks larger' and 'is larger.' Many questions in this domain test whether the model understands that apparent size changes with viewpoint while physical size remains constant. If the answer options mix perceptual and physical language, prefer the interpretation most consistent with the option wording.
- When two children or people of slightly different heights stand at different distances, the shorter one can be made to appear as tall as the taller one by placing the shorter one closer. This is a direct consequence of linear perspective and does not require any optical illusion.

1448.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Scale Consistency) ---
- Whether an object is appropriately sized for a space depends on whether the object's functional coverage matches the space's requirements. When an appliance, fixture, or piece of furniture's serving range is clearly smaller than the space it is meant to serve—such as a small chandelier in a grand hall or a single small air conditioner in a large restaurant—judge it as 'needs to be larger,' not 'appropriate.'
- Text legibility on a sign or poster depends on the joint scale of the display surface and the font size as projected to the viewer's eye. Making the entire poster larger—not just walking closer or enlarging only the font—is often the correct remedy when text is too small to read at the intended viewing distance.
- A container's carrying capacity depends on its three-dimensional internal volume—depth, width, and opening—not just its visible footprint or shape. A deep narrow basket can hold far more than a wide shallow bowl. Differently shaped containers such as triangular versus circular boxes cannot be compared without knowing internal volume. When volume data is unavailable, the comparison may be non-measurable.
- For seating capacity, estimate each table's perimeter and divide by roughly 60 cm per person. A long banquet table seats 5+ per side. Two such tables easily accommodate 10+ people. Do not default to small fixed numbers like 4 per table without visual evidence of a small table.
- A baby or toddler is physically small and light enough to sit on a medium-to-large dog from a pure size-and-scale standpoint. A tree's canopy of leaves inflates its visual bulk far beyond its trunk's solid mass. A bird's retractable neck appears short when folded but extends to considerable length. These are common biological scale facts that override naive visual impressions.
- Industrially manufactured items on the same production line—spools, rollers, bobbins, cable drums, water dispensers, dinnerware sets—are almost always identical in size. Apparent differences in images are caused by perspective, angle, or occlusion. Default to 'same size' unless there is explicit evidence of mixed product types or different batches.
- Stairs that appear smaller at the top of a staircase are the same physical size as the bottom steps; the shrinkage is perspective. Fruits of the same variety at the same stall are typically sorted to similar sizes. Miniature scenes and model displays use artificially scaled props—do not treat their proportions as representing real-world scale relationships.
- When a question asks whether an object can 'fit inside' another, consider all possible placements: inside, on a shelf, on top, or on a front ledge. Read all answer options to identify the specific placement described. For stacking compatibility of appliances, kitchen and laundry machines follow standardized dimension ranges and are often stackable.
- A background or backdrop larger than the foreground object typically makes the foreground object appear more prominent and visually bigger through framing contrast—not smaller. A road's spaciousness depends on designed lane capacity, not momentary traffic congestion. Cloud coverage over a field depends on dynamic wind and weather factors not determinable from a single photo.
- A standard sedan is roughly 2–3 times the length of a scooter. Sugarcane juice yield depends on cross-sectional thickness, not just length. Sweets and pastries can be shaped to dimensions comparable to common household objects like drinking glasses. If all identical objects are scaled up by the same factor, their relative size ordering is preserved—equals remain equal.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Relative Size Comparison) ---
- PRE-CALCULATED spatial relationships reflect apparent image-plane size, not physical size. Objects of the same category at different depths—such as cows in a field, dancers in rows, or square tops on a surface—often appear different sizes due to perspective, but are physically similar. Same-category animals in a herd or group typically have only minor individual size variation; 'mostly similar' is usually more accurate than 'all different.'
- A foreground object that visually overlaps a distant large object cannot physically cover it. The scale gap between nearby small objects and distant large objects is usually enormous. Flowers from a small tub could cover only a tiny branch of a background tree—not half, not most, just a small part. Always estimate the physical scale ratio before choosing among graded coverage options.
- A chain, belt, or rope that wraps around the outside of a wheel has a larger effective radius than the wheel itself, because its path lies beyond the wheel's rim. When PRE-CALCULATED data says the chain is 'smaller,' it is reporting apparent image size, not path radius. The enclosing loop's radius is always greater than the enclosed object's radius.
- Curved transparent containers distort the apparent size of objects inside them. A spherical fishbowl magnifies contents in all directions due to its convex curvature. A cylindrical bowl stretches contents horizontally but not vertically. A flat-sided box-shaped container produces no magnification. When asked which shape makes fish 'look bigger,' cylindrical stretches shape while spherical enlarges overall appearance.
- Moving an object farther from the viewer makes it look smaller; moving it closer makes it look larger. When a question asks 'if object A is taken to object B's location, how will A look compared to C,' recompute A's apparent size at the new depth. A deer moved from the foreground to a distant bin's location will appear smaller than nearby objects.
- A vehicle's interior cargo space minus already-loaded items equals available space. If a van is partly filled with water bottles, the remaining free space may be too small for a puppy even though the entire van is larger than the puppy. When asked 'will X fit inside Y,' subtract existing contents before comparing.
- A shorter background set or backdrop makes the room in front of it appear more expanded and spacious, because the low backdrop reveals more of the actual room behind and above it. It does not make the room look smaller.
- Biological structures have growth limits. A mature flower's inner parts will not grow beyond the outer petals—they remain as they are. Shoes of different sizes differ not only in length but also in width, instep height, and toe box volume. A bird's neck can appear short when folded but extend to considerable length when stretched.
- Physical strength and structural capacity matter for load-bearing questions. A tree, no matter how tall, cannot lift a concrete building—the tree would be crushed under the building's weight. A human body (~1.7 m long) is far too short to span a river or canal that is typically several meters wide. A lighthouse laid horizontally would likely not span a wide canal, and even if it did, it lacks the structural design to serve as a walkable bridge.
- Printed graphics, patterns, or illustrations on a bag, box, or surface create visual illusions of size. A bag with a large book illustration printed on it looks bigger than it actually is. The bag's true internal volume is determined by its physical dimensions, not by the size of its printed decoration. When an option says 'the bag looks bigger due to the image on it,' it is identifying this illusion.
- When comparing an aggregate of smaller objects to a single larger object, mentally reconstruct both at the same depth. A large red stone can be slightly larger than the combined length of three smaller stones stacked above it. Different fruits cut in half have different cross-sectional areas from each other and from whole fruits—the answer is 'different areas for different items,' not a simple true/false about halving.
- Manufactured items of the same specification—square panel tops, table segments, stair treads—are physically identical even when they appear different due to perspective. When asked 'are all X the same size,' and the items are clearly from the same production or construction set, default to 'all equal' unless there is explicit evidence of mixed types.
- When people are sitting, kneeling, or in dance poses, their standing height cannot be determined from their current image height. If the question asks 'will row 1 be the same height as row 2 when standing,' and there is insufficient information about their true standing heights, prefer 'unable to determine' over a definitive yes or no.
- When a table or rectangular object is rotated 90 degrees, its former width becomes its new 'length.' Two tables of the same dimensions oriented differently can have the same length after one is turned. Do not conclude they differ just because they appear different in their current orientations.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Distance-Size Correlation) ---
- Image size is jointly determined by physical size and distance from the camera. A physically smaller person standing closer to the viewpoint can appear the same height as a taller person standing farther away. When a question asks whether repositioning would equalize appearance, the answer depends on how much the depth ratio compensates for the true size ratio.
- Repeated architectural or manufactured elements arranged in depth—such as windows on successive floors, pipes in a row, lamp posts along a road, or cart statues in a plaza—are almost always the same physical size. Their apparent size shrinks with distance purely because of perspective foreshortening, not because the far ones are actually smaller.
- When a question asks 'will object A look the same size as object B if seen from close,' it is asking about projected appearance after hypothetical relocation. If both objects are the same category and type, moving to the same viewing distance will usually reveal equal physical size.
- Distinguish between 'looks larger' and 'is larger.' Many questions in this domain test whether the model understands that apparent size changes with viewpoint while physical size remains constant. If the answer options mix perceptual and physical language, prefer the interpretation most consistent with the option wording.
- When two children or people of slightly different heights stand at different distances, the shorter one can be made to appear as tall as the taller one by placing the shorter one closer. This is a direct consequence of linear perspective and does not require any optical illusion.

1459.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Scale Consistency) ---
- Whether an object is appropriately sized for a space depends on whether the object's functional coverage matches the space's requirements. When an appliance, fixture, or piece of furniture's serving range is clearly smaller than the space it is meant to serve—such as a small chandelier in a grand hall or a single small air conditioner in a large restaurant—judge it as 'needs to be larger,' not 'appropriate.'
- Text legibility on a sign or poster depends on the joint scale of the display surface and the font size as projected to the viewer's eye. Making the entire poster larger—not just walking closer or enlarging only the font—is often the correct remedy when text is too small to read at the intended viewing distance.
- A container's carrying capacity depends on its three-dimensional internal volume—depth, width, and opening—not just its visible footprint or shape. A deep narrow basket can hold far more than a wide shallow bowl. Differently shaped containers such as triangular versus circular boxes cannot be compared without knowing internal volume. When volume data is unavailable, the comparison may be non-measurable.
- For seating capacity, estimate each table's perimeter and divide by roughly 60 cm per person. A long banquet table seats 5+ per side. Two such tables easily accommodate 10+ people. Do not default to small fixed numbers like 4 per table without visual evidence of a small table.
- A baby or toddler is physically small and light enough to sit on a medium-to-large dog from a pure size-and-scale standpoint. A tree's canopy of leaves inflates its visual bulk far beyond its trunk's solid mass. A bird's retractable neck appears short when folded but extends to considerable length. These are common biological scale facts that override naive visual impressions.
- Industrially manufactured items on the same production line—spools, rollers, bobbins, cable drums, water dispensers, dinnerware sets—are almost always identical in size. Apparent differences in images are caused by perspective, angle, or occlusion. Default to 'same size' unless there is explicit evidence of mixed product types or different batches.
- Stairs that appear smaller at the top of a staircase are the same physical size as the bottom steps; the shrinkage is perspective. Fruits of the same variety at the same stall are typically sorted to similar sizes. Miniature scenes and model displays use artificially scaled props—do not treat their proportions as representing real-world scale relationships.
- When a question asks whether an object can 'fit inside' another, consider all possible placements: inside, on a shelf, on top, or on a front ledge. Read all answer options to identify the specific placement described. For stacking compatibility of appliances, kitchen and laundry machines follow standardized dimension ranges and are often stackable.
- A background or backdrop larger than the foreground object typically makes the foreground object appear more prominent and visually bigger through framing contrast—not smaller. A road's spaciousness depends on designed lane capacity, not momentary traffic congestion. Cloud coverage over a field depends on dynamic wind and weather factors not determinable from a single photo.
- A standard sedan is roughly 2–3 times the length of a scooter. Sugarcane juice yield depends on cross-sectional thickness, not just length. Sweets and pastries can be shaped to dimensions comparable to common household objects like drinking glasses. If all identical objects are scaled up by the same factor, their relative size ordering is preserved—equals remain equal.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Shadow-Size Projection) ---
- A shadow's size on a receiving surface is not a simple copy of the casting object's size. It depends on three factors: the distance from the light source to the object, the distance from the object to the surface, and the angle of incidence. Moving an object closer to the light source enlarges its shadow; moving it farther away shrinks it.
- When a shadow is cast onto a surface that is not parallel to the object's plane—such as a wall receiving a shadow from a horizontal wire, or a floor receiving a shadow from a vertical window grille—the shadow is typically elongated or distorted rather than being a same-scale silhouette.
- Outdoor shadows from a low sun angle are almost always longer than the casting object's height or width. The lower the sun, the more elongated the shadow becomes. Overhead midday sun produces shadows that are shorter than the object.
- Tall buildings cast wide shadow regions that can darken large areas of ground and neighboring structures. The shadow area of a tall building is determined by both its height and the sun's elevation angle, so a moderately wide but very tall building can still cast a shadow broad enough to shade an entire street.
- Window-gap and grille projections onto interior walls or floors are commonly enlarged or stretched near one edge because the light enters at an oblique angle. The shadow pattern at the top or far edge of the projection is typically wider than the physical gap that created it.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Perspective Distortion) ---
- Perspective and contextual contrast can change perceived size without changing true size. A human standing next to a giant sculpture or mural appears small by comparison, but remains physically standard-sized. When asked 'is this human smaller than actual size,' the correct answer usually acknowledges that the human is normal-sized and only the reference object is unusually large.
- When small objects of the same type are grouped together, a standard-sized object placed among them will appear larger by contrast. Conversely, a small object placed among large ones will appear even smaller. This is the Ebbinghaus illusion and it affects perceived—not actual—size.
- A shape viewed under perspective foreshortening can appear trapezoidal even if it is actually rectangular or square. A cart roof, table top, or tile pattern that looks like a trapezoid in a photograph may be a rectangle in reality, distorted only by the camera's viewpoint.
- When identical clips, cups, bottles, or tiles are arranged at different positions in the image, those closer to the camera or at image edges may appear larger. Before concluding a true size difference, check whether the objects are the same product or mold. If they are, the size difference is apparent, not real.
- Front-row members in a group photo, parade, or formation appear taller and larger than back-row members purely because they are closer to the camera. This does not mean front-row individuals are actually taller. Perspective exaggerates the size of nearer subjects relative to farther ones.
